title: African tech entrepreneur shares story and career advice for young professionals #techpeepsdiaspora

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: Hello everyone and welcome to another episode of TechPeeps Diaspora. Um, our guest on the show today is Richard Brand. Um, he's a tech entrepreneur who has made a lot of impact in the African tech ecosystem. Um, he has mentored a lot of, um, upcoming young, um, tech professionals in Africa like myself, and he's also very well known as someone who established a talent pool of, um, upcoming software developers, um, in Ghana for the past 3 to 5 years, um, which is also now being extended to other parts of Africa as well. Um, Richard, I'm very happy to have you on the show today.

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: Yeah, hello Zach. Yeah, I'm really happy to be here.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: Um, so Richard, can you tell us a little bit more about yourself and your background and how you started your journey, um, until this point?

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: Um, so my, um, name is Richard Brant and I'm the founder and CEO of Code Train. Um, and what Code Train does is simply to, um, train professional software developers, uh, and then find ways that they can start a career. So we match a lot of our developers, um, that graduate our program to companies to start their careers. And the main aim is to unlock, um, the potential of people, um, here in, in Ghana and, and in other African countries, especially in West Africa, because, um, they have a lot of potential. They are smart just like anybody else. However, they have limits, uh, limitations when it comes to opportunities and, um, a world-class training, the kind of training that can actually and make them compete globally with anybody else in the world. Yeah. So we, we try to get them to, you know, compete globally, um, give them the right skills, the right work ethic, um, to be able to, um, be as good as anybody else. Um, yeah. So basically, um, brief, like, intro— introduction of myself. Um, with my background, I basically, um, Um, did, um, my undergrad, uh, in mathematics. Um, and then, um, I went into, um, tech, um, at the Meltwater Entrepreneurial School of Technology. Um, for 2 years I studied how to become a tech entrepreneur, which I am now. Um, we basically studied, um, how to build, um, how to code, how to build apps. Um, how to pitch to investors, um, and how to start a, a startup. Um, and then after that, I got the opportunity to go to, um, a very big accelerator in, in Santiago de Chile in South America, um, for an 8-month, um, training, um, program and tech accelerator program. Um, and during that time I learned a lot of, um, other skills to advance my, my knowledge and my experience as well. Um, and then, but while I was there, anytime people wanted to hire developers, they on— they always, or anytime people want to do something extraordinary or they wanted to start a, a startup or a company, they always wanted a software developer. And I realized that there are not many, um, people, there's a shortage of software developers and people that can do things. Um, but at the same time, there isn't a shortage of smart people back home. Um, so when I came back home, I, you know, started helping, um, a group of young people, um, to be able to, um, you know, enter into the space and also become software developers and, um, also, you know, be able to, um, fulfill their potential. And that eventually turned into Code Train.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: Um, yeah, that's, um, really interesting. I think, um, I mean, I'm very familiar with, um, what Code Train does because, um, also like someone that, um, came through Code Train. And maybe for people that do not know, can you tell us a bit more about, um, the Code Train program itself? Um, and maybe for upcoming software developers, how they can, you know, benefit from such a program?

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: Um, so, so Code Trainee is basically a coding bootcamp. Um, it's, it's a 1-year program. Um, and then after the 1-year training program, um, we follow up with a 3-month, um, career service program during which we help our students to, um, actually learn how to, you know, um, do interviews. We introduce them to companies and We assist them to, um, get jobs or internships. Um, the main thing, you know, that we try to, um, do in addition to that is to, uh, run it in a way that the students don't feel like they're in a classroom, but then they feel like they're working in a startup. So, um, so that they can actually easily transition to, um, Um, the world of, of work. Um, one thing that is also very important that we also do is, um, to get them to think globally, um, to get them to think, you know, have a global mindset, um, to get them to, um, learn and work with, um, very, um, good work ethic. Um, you know, and also to have very, you know, good values as well. Um, you know, we work on those as well to make sure that they're world-class. Um, and, um, so far it's, it's been, it's been great. Um, 89% of our students actually, um, you know, found jobs in the first 9 months after graduation. Um, 80% of them found jobs in, in the first 6 months. Um, 31% of them found jobs even before Um, they, they graduated. Um, and it's, it's been amazing. We've trained over 500 developers. Um, and we are hoping to, um, to expand the program to include other courses as well. And, but as it stands now, um, we've been able to create, um, the program such that anybody from any background can be able to Um, start their career in tech. And, um, there, there is a high, you know, shortage of software developers globally. There's a very big demand which is not being filled. Um, and you know, um, Africa has the youth, the biggest youth population. Um, and even that is supposed to double by 2050. Um, so there's a need to create, um, um, very good, um, training programs. And, um, that has very, very good, um, um, outcomes when it comes to, um, careers and employment. And, um, so far we've, we've been able to help, um, so many young people here in Ghana to start careers in, in tech. And we've, we've also had, um, every time we get students from other African countries that also, um, benefit from our program. And we have, um, a virtual program as well. Um, so people sit in, even we have a, a student in France. Um, you know, we have a student in Qatar, um, that just like joined, um, the program and they, they do really well. Um, we also, for those in Ghana, we have a weekend class and a weekday class for the in-person, um, in-person bits. Um, and, um, it's, it's been, it's been great so far. The thing I like about The whole thing is it's, um, it's, we are creating a new, a new way of, um, education here, um, that is based on, um, you know, like respect, um, is based on good values, um, is based on responsibility and is based on, um, what, um, young people want. Um, because most other programs are actually designed by, um, people, um, you know, older people, um, for the youth. Um, and, you know, the youth, you know, really do not relate to it well because they have different mindsets. Um, but Code Train is, is, um, a program that is actually, um, designed by young people and, um, run by young people. Um, and, um, even the students also become co-creators and they keep giving feedback on the program so that it meets their expectations. And also to employers also give their feedback with the students that we hire. So I believe that, you know, the approach we've taken could actually become a new way of education in Africa. And we can, if we are successful with that, it could actually Um, answer the question of how we can unlock, um, people's potential and, and not just get them to survive, but, um, get them to actually fulfill, um, their potential. I mean, in, in, in Africa. Hmm.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: It's, um, interesting. Um, you know, when you spoke about, um, you know, the fact that most graduates from Code Train were able to easily Um, you know, find employment or get jobs. And in Africa, or especially in Ghana, you hear so much about like the high unemployment rates. You have so many like university graduates, um, who are still unable to find jobs. And sometimes for me, like, I, um, wonder if, you know, I, I know that there are a lot of like opportunities in tech, and sometimes I wonder why um, people are not taking advantage of this, um, you know, these opportunities? What do you think, um, you know, is the cause of, you know, like there's so much unemployment rate and yet still there's— there are so many opportunities in tech and people are not taking advantage of this? Do you think that it's because maybe at least for software development, um, people think that it's something which is really difficult and Um, you know, they can't do it, or maybe people are just not aware of these opportunities out there.

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: I just, uh, think that there isn't enough information out there. Um, because, um, even myself before I went into tech, um, I mean, everybody thinks that it is for people that do, um, I mean, people still have the mindsets here that, you know, people what you study in school, um, is what you do, you know, the kind of work that you do or the kind of area that you go to. Um, and, um, you know, so I, I feel that, uh, everything starts from the education system. Um, in terms of careers, what most people know or what is out there is actually far from reality, you know, in terms of careers. And it's also based on the way our education is structured where right from high school, uh, you know, someone chooses to do business, you know, and they have to do accounting and other courses and they feel that they want to become an accountant, you know? Um, so I, and, uh, you wait till you get to the university to choose computer science or computer engineering so that you become a software developer. So then the person that did, um, accounting or did business or, and, or did arts or visual arts would think that it is off their limits. You know, it's, it's not something for them. Um, but you know, aware enough awareness should be created that the, the tech industry, um, you know, doesn't, isn't really only limited for, to people that do those courses, but, you know, people with, from different backgrounds could actually, um, start and actually succeed. Um, and a lot of people that are in tech didn't have that background. I think Elon Musk has a degree in physics also. Um, most of, most of them don't have that. Some of them don't even go to college, you know? Um, so, you know, we, we've had high school students that, And right after CodeTrains program, um, they start, they, they shoot their careers with local and international companies. And some of them have even been, um, promoted into senior roles and senior developer roles. Um, we, we even have, um, a student that is still in, in junior high school. Um, and you know, he's, he's actually working on a project to connect, um, investors with, Um, with, with startups and, you know, he's even almost done with the app, you know, and he could even become a, a tech entrepreneur, um, at that age. Um, or could join like a high-growth startup, you know, at any moment. So I, I, you know, there isn't much information, um, around that and it's something that we, we strive to do at Code Train and we do lots of bootcamps in universities to educate people about this. Um, and we, we try to like, you know, hold other, um, sessions and workshops in schools and just try to spread the word, um, so that they can get this opportunity. You know, they can. And then the next thing, the next reason why they're not taking advantage is, um, I think it's simply the belief in, you know, that they can actually, um, succeed, you know? And, um, once again, the system is not, built here in such a way that, um, you know, one thing that is missing in, in, in the training of, um, young people here is, um, to make them believe in themselves and to make them, um, you know, have the ability to actually, um, sit down and like plan and create roadmaps for themselves and solve problems. Um, so, and the tech industry is basically is, purely by merit, uh, most of the time. And, um, you know, it's, it's not based on, um, people getting certificates and being entitled to, or people being entitled to, um, to things. But, you know, it doesn't really discriminate. You know, it's everything is based on, um, on, um, merit. So, um, it's very difficult for people that come from backgrounds where Um, they are being asked to write exams to get a piece of paper so that they can merit something, you know, now have to go into this industry where they have to work for everything. Um, it, it, it becomes a little bit, um, more difficult. But, and once, uh, you know, I think once again we need to create, um, in the minds of young people to believe in themselves so that they can actually, um, firstly, um, feel confident to pursue whatever they want to pursue. Um, it's not only tech, but you know, they should feel that they should be able to, um, succeed in whatever they want to, um, any area of, or any endeavor, um, that they choose to, um, carry out.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: I know that currently, you know, CodeTrain, um, trains, um, several developers, like mainly software developers. Um, yeah, I mean, for people that um, you know, are not really, um, familiar with what opportunities there are in the tech space. Are there any like other roles in, in the tech space that, you know, people can explore apart from just being like a software developer? Or for someone that doesn't want to be like a programmer or a software developer, are there any other roles they can explore?

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: Yeah, so I think, yeah, once again, it's, yeah, we need to create, we need to like create a lot of awareness. So, um, the, the, the tech space alone has, um, so many career paths. And I, I, once again, when you talk to most young people in high school, um, and even the universities, like it's quite surprising that, um, up to now the only, um, careers they know is the traditional ones that has always been there. Doctor, lawyer, you know, engineer, you know, they still say those same things. Um, but there are, you know, in the tech industry alone, there are so many careers. Um, there are so many, um, new careers in, um, um, um, you know, like digital jobs, you know, or digital careers in, um, you know, um, In, in, in today's world. And one great thing is that there is always, there is always, um, um, new even rules coming up, you know, like new rules coming up. Um, I mean, according to the World Economic, um, Forum, so many of the, um, the old, you know, um, careers and roles are even going to fade out, um, making way for, um, so many like roles in, in digital. Um, and I think over 97 million jobs are, you know, are being created, um, in that process. And, um, um, there are other roles, uh, you know, um, you know, you have like DevOps engineers, you have, um, normal software engineer, uh, frontend engineer, or like backend engineer. And then you have like a UI person that designs, um, the app, how it's gonna look like before it's actually built. Um, it's also a very, um, good career to pursue. Um, you have, um, um, um, people that, um, test apps, like app testers too. Um, and you know, they, they test apps and ask feedback from customers and, you know, they create, they kind of like validate like ideas and like the apps and get feedback. Um, you know, you have, um, people that are business analysts that, um, you know, um, talk to, um, um, they, they kind of like talk to, um, um, clients and also like talk to, um, um, uh, management about new products and service being built and try to make sure that there is, um, the requirements are, you know, well, um, done. Um, there are, you know, um, data analysts, um, you know, as well. Um, you have like, um, um, big data specialists. Um, and then you have like, um, digital marketing, um, people and, um, strategy and specialists, you know. Um, you have, um, um, Um, security, um, you know, people that are just doing cybersecurity and security things to make sure that like there is security. Um, I mean, basic designers, like animators. Um, you have like people that, you know, just create content because once you build an app or, and every company now, and the interesting thing is these roles are not only limited to, um, you know, the tech industry now. Because, uh, every bank is a little bit like, every bank is a tech, tech company now, you know? And they all, they all have apps, they all have tech products. Um, every organization, even NGOs, schools, hospitals, banks, you know, all these organizations actually need, um, these people, you know, to be able to run, um, effectively. Um, so yeah, there is, there is so much, I mean, Um, so it's not only limited to, um, it's not only limited to people that code, but, um, there are so many roles that, um, does not require coding, but it, it requires you to be in tune with, you know, what is happening, what the industry is and everything, you know? And there are people that just specialize in building like online ads and even offline, like normal TV ads and for tech companies, you know? Um, we have like creatives too. Um, you know, there's the investment side too. Um, people that go into like tech investment and they work in investment firms and, you know, they specialize in like, you know, software as a service or like, you know, funding like scalable startups. And then you have, um, um, you know, organizations that facilitate the whole industry like hubs, incubators, accelerators. And some of them even work on policy, you know? So for instance, now there, there, there's a team in Ghana right now, um, in collaboration with the GIZ working on, um, Ghana's tech, um, policy, for instance, you know? And then there are so many incubators and, you know, hubs, hubs that actually provide, um, um, office space and the right environments for, um, for tech people. And, you know, so people can enter into the tech industry and work with such hubs. Some of them hold training programs. Um, you know, they need people to run operations. You know, they need people, um, to, you know, like, um, help their, their, you know, their startups and their companies. And so there is so much happening in that space now. Like, you see, even in finance, there's fintech, and where like a lot of finance guys, you know, Um, startups, um, banks, you know, everybody is involved in, like, in, in this space trying to make, um, solve problems like with finance. There's agritech as well. Um, there's also even, um, um, um, you know, um, the people that move things around, um, uh, you know, those mobility companies and, you know, um, companies that provide infrastructure. And, you know, people that have experience in, um, um, in, in such things could also, you know, go into them because, um, most of, um, those comp— the tech industry moves on the infrastructure those services create, such as, um, you know, food delivery services, you know, um, um, um, you know, services that has to do with like moving goods and moving things around, uh, the supply chain, um, you know, shops, like so many things. Uh, and even now events also, you know, it has, it's also in it. There's some, I think after COVID, um, all events were held online. Um, you have like, so it's a whole industry now, you know? Um, so yes. Um, it's not, you know, the coding part is just a small part. Um, but there is, there is something for everyone and the industry actually needs people that Um, are actually good at, you know, something, you know, and sometimes people ask me that, um, where, you know, what is the best thing to do? You know, like what is the hotcake? But, um, I mean, the best answer to that is don't look at going into something which like sounds like, hey, if I go here, I'm going to get a job or I'm going to get opportunities. Um, you know, the industry and the whole world needs people that are very good at what they do. You know? Um, so think about how, um, you can follow your own curiosity to become the master in whatever you, your interest is, right? People will pay you for your value, you know, because people are always looking for such people and are very good at something, right? So you can look at something in the industry that you feel you can do, um, very well. And to you it's not work, But to you it is play, you know, and you can really, um, unlock that potential and people will start paying you not based on what you can do, but based on your judgment, you know, because your judgment can provide so much value. So yeah, so I, I would advise people to just look at, um, not even, even though there's so much opportunities, not try to, um, be outsmart themselves by looking at what, um, is hotcake or what, but they should rather think about it from their angle. Um, how to optimize their, um, their strengths. Besides, um, in the tech industry, people work in teams, you know, and teams are created with, with all these elements, you know? So there isn't something that, you know, that is, you could say it's in hot demand or not because, um, you know, there is, it's, it's people work in teams and all angles are drawn into, into teams. Um, to achieve, um, objectives.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: Um, that's, um, really interesting. One other thing that, um, you know, I'm curious about is, um, you know, you've trained, um, lots of software engineers, um, I mean through CodeTrains program and also as someone who has been in, like, who has been very active in the African, you know, tech scene, you've seen a lot of like engineers start their careers and stuff. What would you say, um, like let's say for someone that's just getting started, what are the main things that separate people who become successful from the ones who don't?

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: Reliability. Just be reliable. You know, I just think that, yeah, you just should be reliable. Like, and, um, like, and you can divide it into little, uh, smaller chunks. I mean, the first chunk is, um, that whatever you want to achieve, um, starts by, so for instance, if you want to become a digital marketer and a content strategist, right, for companies, assume that you are already a digital marketer and a content strategist. Assume that you've already achieved your, your goal. Right? So what is left is for you to just be, um, um, accountable to, to yourself, you know? So you set roadmaps and you set plans and you set objectives and you set milestones and you just, um, achieve those milestones. Because if you, if you are not objective, if you can't, um, you can't be accountable to yourself, it's, it's hard, you know, to be accountable to people. Um, so yeah, so, you know, firstly be reliable, like, but how to break that down is firstly, Um, try to, um, be accountable to yourself. And then secondly, um, that is when you are learning. Um, but then when you start your career, um, with people and with companies, um, you just try to be reliable. Like, just be reliable, you know, and be accountable. Um, and be, be as honest as possible. You can't be perfect, you know, but when you are reliable, people trust you. You know, I think that's, that's a single most important thing. And all the people that I've known that have, um, succeeded, they are just reliable. They just, they get things done. And even the weakest, you know, the weakest person that is reliable is, is better than the most talented person that is not reliable, you know, because in the world of work, um, with people, companies, or, you know, CEOs and, you know, management need people to get things done. You know, and most of the time nobody cares, you know, the, the level at which it's done, but they want it to be done, you know, to make sure that it makes sense, you know, and they need reliable people to, to get it done. And over time, um, these reliable people become specialists and, you know, they hit like very, very high levels in, in their work. But, um, if I have a board meeting next week and I, I just, and I need something done, I just need it done. You know? Um, yeah. So having reliable people is, is for me the most important, um, the most important thing. It's not based on talent or how good someone is. And because with that you can teach, you know, and with that, with experience, you become good. Um, but being reliable is, it's, it's, it's something that draws from the person itself. So, um, it's, it's very hard to do business with people that are not Um, not, not reliable.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: I wasn't expecting the answer, but, um, yeah, myself I see how important like that is. So what's your like happiest moment, um, at Code Train? Like I imagine how challenging it is running something like Code Train, but, uh, are there any like happy moments, um, that you always look back on it and know, it keeps you going and you want to continue doing what you do?

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: Um, I think my happiest moment was when our first, um, like, um, student got a job, you know, and, um, that was the— I mean, still, that feeling was, was the best feeling even up to now. And, um, and it, it meant a lot because it— I mean, If one person could actually, um, be trained by us and, um, get the interest of a company to give them like, you know, an opportunity, means that we can multiply it by, let's say, 100, or, you know, we can multiply it by 1,000. We can make the same impact, 2,000, 3,000. So that actually opened the gateway to, um, everything else that, that happened.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: I think that's all the time we have, but Um, I like for people that, you know, have listened to this and, um, you know, they want to get started on their tech journey, especially for people that want to become software engineers, like how do they start? Like how can they benefit from like Code Train if they want to join?

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: Um, yeah, so I mean, the thing is like when you want to start, um, you know, something Um, it'll be, it's always good to be like in a very good supportive community because it can be very lonely, you know, journey. Um, um, and, um, you know, it's important that they also join, um, um, a, a structured program. Um, so yeah, so we provide that. So we provide a structured program. Um, we also provide like, um, and the community. Um, the interesting thing is now most of our alumni are doing well. Of them are contributing to the African ecosystem, the American ecosystem, the, you know, Canadian, the German, you know, European ecosystems. So we have most of them getting lots of experiences and, um, it's also a great network to also, um, be in. Um, and I mean, to get started, they can just, um, you know, we, we do admissions 3 times in a year. Um, we, we, we run, um, a 1-year bootcamp in software engineering where we do everything. We do frontend, backend, um, and mobile as well. Um, we are starting UX and UI. Um, um, we've started that now. Um, and our next admissions is in September. Um, the next one is in January, you know, and then the next one is in June, um, May. We, we admit 3 times in a year. So all that you have to do is to go to codetrain.africa and, um, And just, um, um, apply. And then our admissions team is going to, um, reach out to them. And we have some part scholarships and financial aid as well, and they can apply to that as well. But then they should give, um, put in like their active phone numbers and, you know, emails, and, uh, admissions team is going to, um, set up a call with them to onboard them in the process to start. And then after a year, and then after, after a year, um, we have, um, so the way the program works is after, um, the year, um, they have to pass a technical interview, um, you know, and get some minimum, um, do, you know, a few minimum things in order to, um, get into our, our career program. And, um, so far the last group that did it, like all of them got into it. Um, it's called the MAGIC program and, um, it's a 3-month program after which, um, they're going to demonstrate, um, the apps they've built at Code Train, um, to, um, an audience of employers, uh, which we call the Demo Day. Um, yeah, so I think it's exciting if they join now. I mean, um, in a year's time, you know, they'll be presented to, you know, um, local and international, um, um, employers. And I mean, we, we can, we, that part is the fun part. You know, we can have fun at, at that age, at that time. But for now, they just need to be able to, um, have the confidence to feel that, um, yeah, it's something that, I mean, once they put in the work, um, they're going to get something, um, good out of it.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: Um, thanks a lot for your time. Um, and, um, agreeing to be on the show, it was like, I myself, I learned a lot of things. Um, and maybe in the future we'll be happy to have you again to talk about maybe some specific stuff, more interesting stuff. But once again, like, sure, sure, sure.

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: I'll be happy to come on. Yeah, I really appreciate. Thanks too.
